graph_vector_fulltext.message:
An AI system used to evaluate the credit score or creditworthiness of natural persons (including to determine access to housing loans) is classified as a high-risk AI system, because it determines access to financial resources and essential services such as housing.

On data governance and bias mitigation, the provided excerpt indicates that the Regulation’s data governance requirements apply, and that—exceptionally and only to the extent strictly necessary to ensure bias detection and correction for high-risk AI systems—providers may need to process data for that purpose, reflecting the Regulation’s concern that bias can cause discrimination. The context also links “high-quality data sets” (used for training/testing/validation and assessment of high-risk AI systems) with required data governance and data management practices.

For transparency and explanation toward affected persons, the context only explicitly supports that deployers “inform natural persons,” and that a “right to obtain an explanation” exists but is limited by Union law; it does not provide the specific content/timing/details of those notices or what the explanation must contain for credit-scoring decisions.

The fraud-detection exception does not apply here: the excerpt says AI systems provided for by Union law for detecting fraud in the offering of financial services (and for prudential purposes like calculating capital requirements) should not be considered high-risk; by contrast, credit scoring/creditworthiness evaluation for natural persons is explicitly high-risk, so it remains high-risk even if used in a financial-services context.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_question: An AI system evaluates credit scores for natural persons to determine access to housing loans. Trace: its high-risk classification, the data governance requirements including bias mitigation, the transparency and explanation obligations to affected persons, and whether the fraud detection exception applies.
graph_vector_fulltext.metric_answer:
An AI system used to evaluate the credit score or creditworthiness of natural persons (including to determine access to housing loans) is classified as a high-risk AI system, because it determines access to financial resources and essential services such as housing.

On data governance and bias mitigation, the provided excerpt indicates that the Regulation’s data governance requirements apply, and that—exceptionally and only to the extent strictly necessary to ensure bias detection and correction for high-risk AI systems—providers may need to process data for that purpose, reflecting the Regulation’s concern that bias can cause discrimination. The context also links “high-quality data sets” (used for training/testing/validation and assessment of high-risk AI systems) with required data governance and data management practices.

For transparency and explanation toward affected persons, the context only explicitly supports that deployers “inform natural persons,” and that a “right to obtain an explanation” exists but is limited by Union law; it does not provide the specific content/timing/details of those notices or what the explanation must contain for credit-scoring decisions.

The fraud-detection exception does not apply here: the excerpt says AI systems provided for by Union law for detecting fraud in the offering of financial services (and for prudential purposes like calculating capital requirements) should not be considered high-risk; by contrast, credit scoring/creditworthiness evaluation for natural persons is explicitly high-risk, so it remains high-risk even if used in a financial-services context.

Content: Text Content:
. However, AI systems provided for by Union law for the purpose of detecting fraud in the  offering of financial services and for prudential purposes to calculate credit institutions’ and insurance undertakings’  capital requirements should not be considered to be high-risk under this Regulation. Moreover, AI systems intended  EN OJ L, 12.7.2024 16/144 ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj
----
 brought to the data and allows training of AI systems without the  transmission between parties or copying of the raw or structured data themselves, without prejudice to the  requirements on data governance provided for in this Regulation. (70) In order to protect the right of others from the discrimination that might result from the bias in AI systems, the  providers should, exceptionally, to the extent that it is strictly necessary for the purpose of ensuring bias detection  and correction in relation to the high-risk AI systems,
----
, this Regulation should not  hamper the development and use of innovative approaches in the public administration, which would stand to  benefit from a wider use of compliant and safe AI systems, provided that those systems do not entail a high risk to  legal and natural persons. In addition, AI systems used to evaluate the credit score or creditworthiness of natural  persons should be classified as high-risk AI systems, since they determine those persons’ access to financial resources  or essential services such as housing
----
 and assessment of high-risk AI systems, certain actors, such as providers, notified bodies and  other relevant entities, such as European Digital Innovation Hubs, testing experimentation facilities and researchers,  should be able to access and use high-quality data sets within the fields of activities of those actors which are related  to this Regulation. European common data spaces established by the Commission and the facilitation of data sharing  between businesses and with government in the public interest will be instrumental to provide trustful,
----

chunks.text: . However, AI systems provided for by Union law for the purpose of detecting fraud in the offering of financial services and for prudential purposes to calculate credit institutions’ and insurance undertakings’ capital requirements should not be considered to be high-risk under this Regulation. Moreover, AI systems intended EN OJ L, 12.7.2024 16/144 ELI: http://data.europa.eu/eli/reg/2024/1689/oj

brought to the data and allows training of AI systems without the transmission between parties or copying of the raw or structured data themselves, without prejudice to the requirements on data governance provided for in this Regulation. (70) In order to protect the right of others from the discrimination that might result from the bias in AI systems, the providers should, exceptionally, to the extent that it is strictly necessary for the purpose of ensuring bias detection and correction in relation to the high-risk AI systems,

, this Regulation should not hamper the development and use of innovative approaches in the public administration, which would stand to benefit from a wider use of compliant and safe AI systems, provided that those systems do not entail a high risk to legal and natural persons. In addition, AI systems used to evaluate the credit score or creditworthiness of natural persons should be classified as high-risk AI systems, since they determine those persons’ access to financial resources or essential services such as housing

and assessment of high-risk AI systems, certain actors, such as providers, notified bodies and other relevant entities, such as European Digital Innovation Hubs, testing experimentation facilities and researchers, should be able to access and use high-quality data sets within the fields of activities of those actors which are related to this Regulation. European common data spaces established by the Commission and the facilitation of data sharing between businesses and with government in the public interest will be instrumental to provide trustful,
